cards.text: The smallest unit of memory is a {{c1::bit}}, which can hold a value of {{c2::0}} or {{c3::1}}.

cards.front: How many bits are in a byte in C++?

cards.text: A {{c1::data type}} tells the compiler how to interpret the contents of memory in a meaningful way.

cards.front: What are the main categories of fundamental data types in C++?

cards.text: The {{c1::void}} keyword means {{c2::no type}}. It is primarily used to indicate a function does not return a value.

cards.front: What does the _t suffix mean in type names like std::size_t?
cards.back: The _t suffix means 'type' and is a common naming convention for types defined via typedef or type alias.

cards.text: In C++, an {{c1::object}} is a region of storage that has a {{c2::value}} and other associated properties.

cards.question: Which of these variable declarations is valid?
cards.explanation: C++ is case-sensitive. The type must be lowercase int, not Int or INT.

cards.text: Best practice: Define multiple variables of the same type in {{c1::separate statements}} rather than in a single statement.

cards.concept: Why define variables in separate statements instead of comma-separated
cards.explanation:
Defining multiple variables in one statement is error-prone because:
  • Easy to accidentally mix initialized and uninitialized variables
  • Harder to comment individual variables
  • Can't mix different initialization methods
  • Less readable and maintainable
examples: int a, b;           // bad: multiple in one

int a;
int b;      // good: separate statements
